e8b70787-572a-414b-beee-c4f6b5ca56e0.title: dns
e8b70787-572a-414b-beee-c4f6b5ca56e0.summary:
DNS TROUBLESHOOTING FOR INTERNAL PORTAL ACCESS ISSUES

What this document covers:
This knowledge base article addresses problems where users cannot access company internal portals or web applications, receiving connection errors, timeouts, or access denied messages. The issue is typically caused by Netskope steering policy misconfigurations or DNS resolution failures that prevent proper traffic routing through the Netskope tunnel. The document provides a systematic troubleshooting workflow to identify whether traffic is correctly routed through Netskope.

Key technical concepts:

• Netskope steering policies control which domains route through the Netskope security tunnel versus direct internet connections. Internal portals must be explicitly added to these policies.

• DNS resolution returns IP addresses that indicate routing path. Netskope-routed traffic typically resolves to IP ranges 163.116.x.x or 192.168.x.x, while other IPs suggest traffic is bypassing the tunnel.

• DNS cache can store outdated IP addresses that point to incorrect routing paths, requiring cache flushes on both operating system and network stack levels.

• The Netskope client runs as a system tray (Windows) or menu bar (Mac) application that establishes and maintains the security tunnel connection.

• Traffic bypassing Netskope may be blocked by internal firewall rules that expect all internal portal access to originate from the Netskope tunnel infrastructure.

• mDNSResponder is the macOS DNS resolution service that must be restarted after clearing cache to ensure fresh lookups.

Diagnostic steps and commands:

• Verify Netskope installation status on user device
• Identify operating system (Windows, Mac, or Linux)
• Mac/Linux DNS lookup: host your-internal-portal-url
• Windows DNS lookup: nslookup your-internal-portal-url
• Check if returned IP falls within Netskope ranges (163.116.x.x or 192.168.x.x)
• Disconnect Netskope via system tray/menu bar icon
• Wait 10 seconds and reconnect Netskope
• Re-run DNS check to verify IP change
• Mac DNS flush: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache && sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder
• Windows DNS flush: ipconfig /flushdns

Resolution approaches:

• Restart Netskope tunnel connection to re-establish proper routing
• Clear local DNS cache to remove stale IP address mappings
• Escalate to network team to add domain to Netskope steering policy configuration

When to use this document:
Use this troubleshooting guide when users report inability to access internal company portals or web applications with connection failures. This applies specifically when Netskope is deployed as the security tunnel solution for internal resource access.

8f75bfad-4ca2-42f7-aeb6-1347c531710e.title: testdcl
8f75bfad-4ca2-42f7-aeb6-1347c531710e.summary:
SUMMARY FOR ENGINEERS

What this document covers:
This is a technical design document for establishing a secure site-to-site IPSec VPN between Dubai (10.50.0.0/24) and London (10.60.0.0/24) offices using Cisco ASA 5500-X firewalls at both locations. The solution addresses the absence of dedicated MPLS or private circuits by leveraging existing internet uplinks with cryptographic-grade security for all inter-site traffic including voice, data, and video.

Key technical concepts:

- VPN encryption uses IKEv2 protocol with AES-256 encryption and SHA-256 integrity hashing, meeting NIST SP 800-77 Rev.1 requirements. Forward secrecy is provided through Diffie-Hellman Group (384-bit ECDH). Initial deployment uses pre-shared key authentication with a migration path to PKI certificate-based authentication.

- High availability design implements dual-ISP connectivity at each site with separate IKEv2 tunnels over each ISP path. Dead peer detection (DPD) and SLA monitoring enable sub-60-second tunnel recovery on primary path failure, with automatic failover to backup tunnels.

- Quality of Service implementation uses DSCP marking with VoIP traffic marked as EF (decimal 46) in strict priority queues, and video conferencing marked as AF41 in bandwidth-guaranteed queues. Cisco ASA Modular Policy Framework enforces queuing at outside interface egress.

- MTU optimization sets tunnel MTU at specified bytes to accommodate IPSec header overhead of approximately specified bytes. TCP MSS clamping is enforced to prevent session fragmentation. Expected throughput is 85-90 Mbps on Dubai's 100 Mbps circuit with AES-NI hardware offload on ASA 5525-X.

- Monitoring utilizes SNMPv3 with AES-128 privacy, Netflow v9 for traffic telemetry, and syslog over TLS to centralized SIEM. IPSec MIB polling occurs at 60-second intervals with alerting for tunnel down events, re-key failures, and packet loss exceeding 0.5%.

- VoIP traffic requirements specify under 150ms one-way delay, under 30ms jitter, and under 1% packet loss to maintain MOS above 3.5 per ITU-T G.114 standards. G.711 codec calls generate approximately specified Kbps bidirectional.

Diagnostic steps or commands mentioned:
None explicitly listed in this design document excerpt.

Resolution approaches covered:
- Two-week implementation plan with week one for pre-deployment validation and week two for production deployment
- Rollback procedures defined at each step with maximum rollback time specified
- 48-hour post-cutover stability monitoring window

When to use this document:
Use this document when planning or implementing secure site-to-site VPN connectivity between geographically separated offices over internet circuits where MPLS is cost-prohibitive or has excessive lead times. Reference when designing QoS policies for mixed voice, video, and data traffic over IPSec tunnels.
